title: Metrics & Monitoring — System Design
description: Designing a metrics and monitoring system: collect time-series measurements from thousands of services, store them efficiently, query them for dashboards, and alert when something breaks. The interesting problems are data-intensive: an enormous write volume of timestamped points, time-series storage with downsampling, controlling label cardinality (the silent killer), fast range queries, and reliable alerting. We build from a naive metrics table toward a sharded, downsampled, cardinality-guarded time-series platform with alerting.

functional: Ingest time-series metrics (name + labels + value + timestamp) from many services.
Query metrics over time ranges with aggregation (rate, sum, percentiles, group-by label).
Render dashboards backed by those queries.
Evaluate alert rules and notify when thresholds/conditions are met.
Retain recent data at full resolution and older data downsampled.
nonFunctional: Absorb a huge, continuous write volume (millions of points/sec).
Fast range queries for interactive dashboards (sub-second for recent data).
Bounded storage growth via downsampling and retention.
Control label cardinality so a bad label can't explode the index.
Reliable alerting — don't miss a real alert; avoid storms of false ones.
Graceful degradation: drop/sample under overload rather than fall over.

capacity.label: Ingest rate
capacity.note: Thousands of hosts x many metrics x labels, every few seconds.

capacity.label: Active series
capacity.note: A unique (metric, label-set) is one series; cardinality dominates cost.

capacity.label: Recent query latency
capacity.note: Dashboards need interactive reads over recent data.

capacity.label: Retention
capacity.note: Old data is downsampled, not kept raw.

capacity.label: Write:read
capacity.note: Far more points written than queried, but queries scan wide ranges.

capacity.label: Alert evaluation
capacity.note: Rules run continuously over recent data.

api.description: Push a batch of samples. Fire-and-forget; buffered and written asynchronously.
Range query with aggregation over a time window and step.
Create an alert rule (expression + threshold + duration + routing).

dataModel.name: series
dataModel.note: A unique (metric name + label set). The cardinality unit; indexed for lookup.

dataModel.name: samples
dataModel.note: Append-only (series_id, ts, value) points, stored in time-ordered, compressed chunks.

dataModel.name: rollups
dataModel.note: Pre-aggregated lower-resolution samples (1m, 1h) for older data and fast wide queries.

patterns.name: Time-series storage (chunked, compressed)
patterns.what: Store samples in time-ordered, columnar, delta/double-delta compressed chunks per series.
patterns.whenToUse: Append-mostly timestamped data queried by range.

patterns.name: Downsampling / rollups
patterns.what: Pre-aggregate older data to coarser resolutions and drop raw points.
patterns.whenToUse: Bounding storage and speeding wide-range queries over long history.

patterns.name: Push vs pull collection
patterns.what: Agents push samples, or the system scrapes/pulls targets on a schedule.
patterns.whenToUse: Choose by service-discovery and network topology; pull eases liveness, push eases ephemeral jobs.

patterns.name: Cardinality control
patterns.what: Limit unique label combinations so the series index doesn't explode.
patterns.whenToUse: Always — high-cardinality labels (user id, request id) are the #1 way TSDBs fall over.

patterns.name: Pre-aggregation / read model
patterns.what: Materialize common aggregations so queries don't scan raw samples.
patterns.whenToUse: Dashboards repeatedly running the same heavy aggregations.

patterns.name: Rule-based alerting
patterns.what: Continuously evaluate expressions with a 'for' duration to debounce, then route notifications.
patterns.whenToUse: Turning metrics into actionable, non-flappy alerts.

patterns.name: Sharding by series
patterns.what: Partition series across nodes by a hash of the series id/labels.
patterns.whenToUse: When ingest/query volume outgrows one node.

patterns.name: Work queue / ingestion buffer
patterns.what: Buffer incoming samples so write spikes don't overwhelm storage.

steps.title: 1. Naive Metrics Table (the baseline)
steps.description: Start by writing every metric sample as a row in a relational table and querying with GROUP BY. It works for one service; at millions of points/sec with millions of series it falls over — motivating a purpose-built time-series store.
steps.decisionPrompt: Why does a row-per-sample relational table collapse at metrics scale?

concepts.definition: A sequence of (timestamp, value) points for one metric+label set.
concepts.whyItMatters: Its append-mostly, range-queried shape is unlike OLTP rows.

concepts.definition: Each unique label combination is a distinct series.
concepts.whyItMatters: Cardinality, not raw volume, is what usually kills these systems.

talkingPoints: Name write volume, range queries, and cardinality as the three forces.
Reject a generic RDBMS for the sample store.

traps.trap: Storing each sample as a relational row with a btree index
traps.why: Index maintenance and storage explode; range scans are slow at billions of rows.
traps.instead: Use a time-series store: chunked, compressed, time-ordered per series.

bottlenecks.issue: Row-per-sample writes and indexes don't scale.

strong: Identifies cardinality and range-query patterns immediately.
weak: Treats metrics like ordinary OLTP rows.

whyNow: The baseline exposes why time-series storage and cardinality control are needed.

steps.title: 2. Ingestion Pipeline
steps.description: Accept samples fire-and-forget into a buffer, then write to storage asynchronously. Batching and a buffer absorb spikes and decouple producers from storage. Under overload, sample or shed rather than block producers.
steps.decisionPrompt: 10M points/sec arrive in bursts. How do you absorb them without blocking the services emitting metrics?

concepts.definition: Producers don't wait for durable storage; metrics are best-effort.
concepts.whyItMatters: Monitoring must never slow the systems it watches.

concepts.definition: Queue samples and write in batches.
concepts.whyItMatters: Amortizes write cost and smooths spikes.

options.name: Buffer + async batch writes (default)
pros: Absorbs bursts; batching maximizes write throughput.
cons: Recent samples can be lost on buffer failure (acceptable for metrics).

options.name: Synchronous durable writes
cons: Slow path blocks producers; metrics shouldn't impose that.

traps.trap: Making metric ingestion a blocking, durable write
traps.why: Monitoring overhead then degrades the very services it observes.
traps.instead: Fire-and-forget into a buffer; metrics are best-effort, not transactional.

traps.why: A metrics flood can OOM ingestion and take monitoring down exactly when you need it.
traps.instead: Sample/shed under pressure and protect the buffer with limits.

failureDrills.scenario: Ingestion buffer saturates during an incident.
failureDrills.mitigation: Shed/sample low-priority series; keep alert-critical metrics flowing.

strong: Keeps ingest fire-and-forget and plans overload behavior.

recap.before: Samples hit storage synchronously per row.
recap.after: Buffered, batched, best-effort ingestion that absorbs spikes.
recap.newRisk: Best-effort means possible recent-sample loss; overload policy needed.

whyNow: A resilient intake path must exist before optimizing storage.

steps.title: 3. Time-Series Storage
steps.description: Store samples per series in time-ordered, compressed chunks (delta-of-delta timestamps, XOR-compressed values), with recent data in a hot store and an index from labels to series ids. This is what makes both writes and range reads cheap.
steps.decisionPrompt: How do you store billions of points so both appends and range reads are cheap?

concepts.definition: Group a series' points into time chunks with specialized compression.
concepts.whyItMatters: Cuts storage ~10x and makes range scans sequential.

concepts.definition: labels -> series ids, so queries find matching series fast.
concepts.whyItMatters: Query selection happens on the index; sample scan happens on chunks.

deepDives.title: Why a TSDB beats a generic store
points: Timestamps compress with delta-of-delta; float values with XOR — both near-free for regular intervals.
Append-mostly per-series chunks turn writes and range reads into sequential I/O.
An inverted index maps label matchers to series ids without scanning samples.

traps.trap: Indexing every sample by timestamp in a btree
traps.why: Btree maintenance per point is the bottleneck; you want append-mostly chunks.
traps.instead: Chunk per series with columnar compression; index labels, not samples.

traps.trap: Keeping all data at full resolution forever
traps.why: Storage grows without bound and wide queries scan too much.
traps.instead: Downsample old data into rollups (next step).

failureDrills.scenario: A wide dashboard query spans 30 days at full resolution.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: It stays fast and doesn't scan raw points across a month.
failureDrills.mitigation: Serve long ranges from rollups; raw only for recent windows.

strong: Chooses chunked/compressed per-series storage with a label index.

recap.before: Samples were rows with heavy indexes.
recap.after: Compressed time-ordered chunks + a label index make writes and reads cheap.
recap.newRisk: Full-resolution storage still grows; long ranges need rollups.

whyNow: Storage is the core of a TSDB; everything reads and writes through it.

steps.title: 4. Downsampling & Retention
steps.description: Bound storage and speed wide queries by pre-aggregating older data into coarser resolutions (raw -> 1m -> 1h) and expiring raw points past a retention window. Queries pick the finest resolution that covers the requested range.
steps.decisionPrompt: A user asks for a 90-day CPU graph. What do you actually read?

concepts.definition: Aggregating raw points into lower-resolution buckets (min/max/sum/count).
concepts.whyItMatters: A 90-day graph needs hourly points, not millions of raw ones.

concepts.definition: Raw for days, 1m for weeks, 1h for months.
concepts.whyItMatters: Caps storage while preserving useful history.

traps.trap: Querying raw samples for long time ranges
traps.why: Scanning months of raw points is slow and expensive.
traps.instead: Pick the coarsest rollup that satisfies the step; raw only for recent, fine-grained views.

traps.why: Averages hide spikes — a 1-second outage vanishes in an hourly mean.
traps.instead: Keep min/max/sum/count per bucket so spikes and percentiles survive.

failureDrills.scenario: An alert needs to catch a 5-second error spike but the query hits hourly rollups.
failureDrills.mitigation: Alerts evaluate against recent raw/fine data; rollups serve historical dashboards.

strong: Tiered retention and resolution-aware queries; preserves min/max.

recap.before: All data was raw and wide queries were slow.
recap.after: Tiered rollups bound storage and make long-range reads fast.
recap.newRisk: Downsampling loses detail — keep enough aggregates and alert on fine data.

whyNow: Without downsampling, storage and wide queries grow without bound.

steps.title: 5. Collection: Push vs Pull
steps.description: Decide how samples arrive: agents push to ingestion, or the system pulls/scrapes targets on a schedule via service discovery. Each suits different topologies; many systems do both (pull for long-lived services, push for ephemeral jobs).
steps.decisionPrompt: Pull gives you free liveness checks but struggles with short-lived jobs. When do you push instead?

concepts.definition: The system periodically fetches metrics from discovered targets.
concepts.whyItMatters: A failed scrape is itself a liveness signal; central control of frequency.

concepts.definition: Targets send metrics to a gateway.
concepts.whyItMatters: Works for ephemeral jobs and networks where targets aren't reachable.

options.name: Pull/scrape with a push gateway for ephemeral jobs (default)
pros: Scrape failures = free liveness; central scrape control.
cons: Service discovery needed; firewalled targets are hard to scrape.

options.name: Push-only
pros: Simple for targets; works behind NAT/firewalls.
cons: No free liveness signal; a dead target is silent.
Targets control rate -> harder to throttle centrally.

traps.why: The job finishes before the next scrape, so its metrics are never collected.
traps.instead: Let ephemeral jobs push to a gateway; scrape long-lived services.

traps.why: A silent target may be down — the absence is signal.
traps.instead: Treat scrape failure as a liveness alert, not just missing data.

strong: Picks pull for liveness, push for ephemeral, and uses both.

recap.after: Pull for long-lived services (free liveness) + push gateway for ephemeral jobs.
recap.newRisk: Service discovery and gateway add moving parts.

whyNow: How data arrives shapes liveness detection and the ingestion edge.

steps.title: 6. Cardinality Control
steps.description: Cardinality — the number of unique label combinations — is the silent killer of TSDBs. A single unbounded label (user id, request id, URL with ids) creates millions of series and blows up the index. Guard ingestion: drop, limit, or relabel high-cardinality labels.
steps.decisionPrompt: Someone adds a 'user_id' label to a high-traffic metric. What happens, and how do you prevent it?

concepts.whyItMatters: Memory and index size scale with active series, not sample volume.
It's the most common way these systems OOM and crash.

deepDives.title: Taming cardinality
points: Reject or strip labels with unbounded value spaces (ids, emails, raw URLs) at ingest.
Enforce a per-metric series limit; alert owners when a metric's cardinality spikes.
Offer aggregation at write time (drop the offending label) instead of storing every combination.

traps.why: One bad label (request id) creates millions of series and OOMs the index.
traps.instead: Guard at ingest: cap per-metric cardinality, drop/relabel unbounded labels, alert on spikes.

traps.trap: Putting unbounded ids in label values to 'slice by anything'
traps.why: Metrics aren't a logging/tracing system; high-cardinality belongs in logs/traces.
traps.instead: Keep labels low-cardinality; use logs/traces for per-entity detail.

failureDrills.scenario: A deploy adds a label with per-request ids.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: The index doesn't explode and the system stays up.
failureDrills.mitigation: Cardinality guard drops/limits the new series and pages the metric owner.

strong: Treats cardinality as the primary scaling risk and guards ingest.

recap.after: Cardinality is bounded and monitored at the ingest edge.
recap.newRisk: Guard rules need tuning; legitimate new series must still be allowed.

whyNow: Cardinality is the failure mode interviewers most want addressed; it must be controlled before scaling out.

steps.title: 7. Querying & Alerting
steps.description: A query service evaluates range expressions (rate, percentiles, group-by) over the index + chunks, cached for repeated dashboard queries. An alerting engine continuously evaluates rules with a 'for' duration to debounce, then routes notifications.
steps.decisionPrompt: How do you keep dashboards fast and stop a flapping metric from paging on-call every 10 seconds?

concepts.definition: Reuse results for recurring dashboard queries; materialize common aggregations.
concepts.whyItMatters: Dashboards re-run the same heavy queries on a refresh interval.

concepts.definition: An alert fires only if the condition holds for a sustained period.
concepts.whyItMatters: Prevents a momentary blip from paging on-call.

deepDives.title: Alerting that people trust
points: Evaluate rules on recent fine-resolution data on a fixed interval.
Require the condition to hold 'for' a duration to debounce transient spikes.
Deduplicate and group notifications; route by severity so on-call isn't drowned.

traps.trap: Alerting on a single instantaneous breach
traps.why: Normal jitter pages on-call constantly; people start ignoring alerts.
traps.instead: Use a 'for' duration so only sustained conditions fire.

traps.trap: Re-scanning raw data for every dashboard refresh
traps.why: Identical heavy queries every 15s waste the cluster.
traps.instead: Cache query results and pre-aggregate common expressions.

failureDrills.scenario: A metric flaps above/below threshold every few seconds.
failureDrills.mitigation: 'for' duration + notification dedup/grouping suppress the flapping.

strong: Debounces alerts with 'for' and caches/pre-aggregates queries.
Groups and routes notifications by severity.

recap.before: Data was stored but not usefully queried or alerted on.
recap.after: Fast cached queries and debounced, routed alerting.
recap.newRisk: Cache freshness and rule tuning become ongoing concerns.

whyNow: Storage is only valuable once it drives dashboards and trustworthy alerts.

steps.title: 8. Scaling by Sharding
steps.description: Shard series across nodes by a hash of series id/labels so ingest and queries scale horizontally. A metric router directs samples and queries to the owning shard; queries spanning shards fan out and merge. Replicate shards for availability.
steps.decisionPrompt: Ingest and active series outgrow one node. How do you split the load while keeping a series' data together?

concepts.definition: Hash the series id/labels to a shard so all of a series' samples co-locate.
concepts.whyItMatters: Keeps a series' chunks together for efficient append and range read.

concepts.definition: Fan a multi-series query to relevant shards and merge results.
concepts.whyItMatters: Queries spanning many series must aggregate across shards.

options.name: Hash-shard by series + replicas (default)
pros: Linear ingest/query scaling; a series stays on one shard.
Replicas give availability and read scaling.
cons: Cross-shard queries fan out and merge; hot series/shards need rebalancing.

options.name: Single big node + vertical scale
cons: Hard ceiling on ingest and active series; single point of failure.

deepDives.title: Hot shards and fan-out
points: A few popular metrics can make one shard hot — rebalance or split those series.
Multi-series queries scatter to shards and gather; push aggregation down to each shard first.
Replicate shards so a node loss doesn't lose recent data or block queries.

traps.trap: Sharding by time instead of series
traps.why: Every current write lands on one 'now' shard — a moving hotspot — and a series' data scatters across time shards.
traps.instead: Shard by series so writes spread and a series stays co-located.

traps.trap: Gathering raw points from all shards then aggregating centrally
traps.why: Ships huge volumes over the network.
traps.instead: Push partial aggregation to each shard; merge the small results.

strong: Shards by series, pushes aggregation down, replicates shards.

recap.before: One node capped ingest and series count.
recap.after: Series-sharded, replicated cluster scaling ingest and queries horizontally.
recap.newRisk: Cross-shard queries and hot shards need fan-out and rebalancing.

whyNow: Sharding is the final lever once storage, cardinality, and queries are sound.

finalDesign.title: Final Design
finalDesign.description: End-to-end: agents push/scrape samples into a buffered ingestion path guarded for cardinality; series are hash-sharded across replicated time-series nodes storing compressed chunks with a label index; a rollup worker downsamples into tiered retention; a cached query service answers dashboards (raw for recent, rollups for long ranges); an alerting engine evaluates debounced rules and routes notifications.

functional.how: Query service over label index + chunks, cached.
Rule engine with 'for' debounce and routed notifications.

nonFunctional.how: Buffered async batch writes; series sharding.
Compressed chunks + label index + query cache.
Ingest-time guard: cap, drop, relabel high-cardinality labels.
Debounced rules on fine data, grouped/routed notifications.
Sample/shed under overload; keep alert-critical metrics flowing.

say: Restate it: collect, store, query, and alert on time-series at scale.
List functional (ingest, query, dashboards, alerts, retention) and non-functional (write volume, query speed, cardinality, alert reliability).
Name the three forces: write volume, range queries, and cardinality.
Size points/sec, active series, retention tiers, and alert cadence.
Sketch ingest, query_range, and rule creation.
Start naive to expose TSDB needs, then ingestion buffer, time-series storage, rollups.
Cover collection (push/pull), then cardinality control, then query + alerting.
Go deep where pushed: storage compression, cardinality, or alert debouncing.
Shard by series with replicas; push aggregation down on fan-out.
Summarize requirements and name the dominant risk (cardinality explosion).

expectations: Knows metrics are time-series needing a purpose-built store, not OLTP rows.
Understands dashboards query ranges and alerts watch thresholds.
Designs compressed chunked storage with a label index and tiered downsampling.
Treats ingestion as fire-and-forget with overload handling.
Makes cardinality control a first-class concern at the ingest edge.
Shards by series with fan-out aggregation and replication.
Quantifies storage/query/cardinality tradeoffs and degradation behavior.

followUps: How would you support high-cardinality exploration (exemplars/trace links) without exploding the TSDB?
How do you make alerting itself highly available so it never misses during an outage?
How would you implement long-term storage in object storage with a query layer on top?
How do you handle late-arriving or out-of-order samples?
How would you support multi-tenancy with per-tenant cardinality and query limits?
How do you backfill historical data and recompute rollups after a schema change?
